detailedExemplarData.title: Optimal Placement of Solar Panels for Maximum Efficiency

subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Clear title and aim provide an effective entry point
Table of contents and section headings aid navigation
Conclusion succinctly restates objectives and outcomes
weaknesses: Logical gaps between model choice and justification disrupt coherence
Lengthy background and repeated citations reduce conciseness
Inconsistent pagination in the table of contents
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the report has visible structure (TOC, headings, conclusion) and relevant material, several logical jumps and unnecessary background bulk limit coherence and conciseness. On balance this merits a low but non-zero mark.

strengths: Effective use of tables and diagrams to illustrate data and models
Transitions to new model phases are signposted
weaknesses: Frequent notation errors (sin sin duplication, missing units)
Inconsistent values (contradictory optimal angles, mis-copied formulas)
Graphical elements lack clear labels and embedded plots
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student uses representations and links them to text, but pervasive inconsistencies in notation, calculation and formatting undermine clarity. The overall limited consistency justifies a low band.

strengths: Personal motivation drawn from household context is clearly articulated
Student collects local data and shapes a bespoke model
Demonstrates initiative in applying real-time measurements
weaknesses: Choice of sampling times is unexplained
Further creative extensions (alternative models) are not explored
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is driven by genuine personal interest and shows significant independent thinking and initiative. However, it stops short of outstanding creativity or fully justifying methodological choices.

strengths: Identifies the form of the quadratic model
weaknesses: Reflection is largely descriptive with little critical depth
Fails to evaluate whether a quadratic fit is appropriate
Connections to broader mathematical understanding are superficial
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reflection is present but remains basic and descriptive, lacking meaningful critique or strong links to learning, matching only the lowest reflective band.

strengths: Uses SL-level trigonometry and elementary calculus appropriately
Data evaluation at multiple angles demonstrates understanding of model behavior
weaknesses: Multiple computational and algebraic errors (derivative, cosine formulas)
Optimal angle result lies outside the practical domain without discussion
Rationale for quadratic fit lacks statistical justification
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The mathematics is relevant and at course level, showing some correct application. However, frequent errors and lack of rigour limit the work to a low mid-band score.

strandSelections.reasoning: Selecting local data, deriving a custom model and attempting optimisation show significant independent thinking even though execution is flawed.  Band 2 is therefore warranted.

The student demonstrates a basic grasp of trigonometry and optimisation but explanations reveal misconceptions (e.g. handling of constants, quadratic fitting rationale).  Hence only âsome understandingâ is evidentâband 2.
Several computational mistakes (incorrect cosine formula, derivative error, contradictory optimal angles) mean mathematics is only partially correct.  Band 2 captures this partial accuracy.

While most material relates to tilt optimisation, lengthy background paragraphs on photovoltaic basics and repeated citation lists add unnecessary bulk.  Hence the exploration is âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elements,â fitting band 2.

Correct symbols (Î², Î¸, Î±) are used but frequently duplicated as âsin sin,â units are omitted, and derivative notation is mishandled.  Appropriate language appears only intermittently, matching band 2.

Explanations are generally understandable but internal contradictions (two different âoptimalâ angles, mis-copied formulas) reduce consistency.  This is âmostly clear with some consistency,â i.e., band 2.

Trigonometric relations and elementary calculus used are at the SL course level and generally relevant.  The work is not sophisticated, so band 3 (appropriate course-level mathematics) is most fitting.
The student employs tables, a scatter/line graph, and three explanatory diagrams, all clearly linked to the text.  Although sophistication is limited, the use is effective, justifying band 3.
Reflection is brief and mainly descriptive; it lacks depth on the mathematical process or personal learning.  This meets only the âlimited reflectionâ descriptor (band 1).
Collecting real-time data and constructing a bespoke quadratic model exhibit meaningful initiative, though creativity does not reach the âoutstandingâ level.  Band 2 is appropriate.

The student follows a recognizable path: introduction â data collection â modelling â optimisation â conclusion.  Nevertheless, several logical gaps remain (e.g. the sudden choice of a quadratic fit, unexplained misuse of trigonometric identities, conflicting optimal-angle values).  These breaks prevent smooth development, so the work is only âsomewhatâ coherent, aligning with band 2.
The student notes weather as a limitation but offers no substantive critique of model validity or data reliability.  This is basic analysis with limited critique, fitting band 1.
Connections between findings and enhanced mathematical understanding are asserted rather than demonstrated; thus only a limited link is evident, consistent with band 1.

A clear table of contents, section headings, numbered tables/figures and a concluding section give the report a solid three-part structure (intro/body/conclusion).  Flow is adequate though not seamless, therefore band 3 is most appropriate.
The investigation is motivated by the studentâs household experience; this personal drive shapes the exploration and data choice, satisfying the descriptor for a clear personal approach (band 2).

annotations.comment: The title is prominently displayed and clearly communicates the focus of the exploration, setting an effective first impression.
The table of contents provides a clear overview of sections with page numbers, aiding reader navigation and demonstrating solid report structure.
Page numbers in the table of contents do not align with actual report pagination (e.g., References on 17 vs 16). Please update the TOC for consistency.
The studentâs personal motivation and household context are clearly articulated, showing genuine engagement with the topic.
The aim and plan are clearly stated, outlining data sources, mathematical concepts, and optimization goals in a coherent manner.
The data collection section lists three timeâpoints without justification. Consider explaining why these specific times were selected for the analysis.
Solar altitude values in Table 1 exceed realistic bounds (e.g., 173.85Â°), indicating a conversion or data entry error. Please verify the raw data and units.
Footnotes for references 3 and 4 are duplicated and formatting is inconsistent. Please ensure each citation is uniquely labeled and formatted uniformly.
Figure 1 lacks clear labels for critical components (axes, angles). Annotating angle of incidence and tilt would improve reader comprehension.
The sentence âI considered the Earth as a spherical and appear at a relatively low positionâ¦â is grammatically unclear. Please revise to clarify the spatial assumption.
The azimuth angle is denoted by â, but using the emptyâset symbol may confuse readers. Consider the standard Greek Ï notation.
The introduction contains extensive background on photovoltaics, which reduces conciseness. Consider trimming general context to focus more on the mathematical objectives.
Transitioning to an energy output model as a function of Î² is well signposted, aiding reader understanding of the model development.
E(Î²) notation in the block is crowded; grouping terms with brackets and spacing around operators will improve readability.
The constant K is defined as â1366. 1 Wmâ»Â²â with an extra space and period. Please correct number formatting and units.
Energy evaluations at Î²=2,10,20,â¦ effectively demonstrate model behavior across angles, providing a solid dataset for fitting.
In entries CâE the same cosine factors for Î² and Ï are reused, suggesting a copy-paste error. Recalculate each case with correct angle values.
Table 2 shows Î²=10 as E=546.726, but earlier it was 546.85. This inconsistency undermines accuracy; please reconcile values.
You mention generating a characteristic graph in Excel but no image is included. Embedding this plot would support the choice of regression model.
The rationale for selecting a quadratic fit is not justified with regression statistics or residual analysis. Please include model-fit metrics.
The definition of Î¸ is given, but its connection to the later energy model is not made explicit. Adding a brief link would strengthen logical flow.
The proportionality âE â cos Î¸â lacks specified units and context for the constant of proportionality. Please include units or refer to the constant K clearly.
The formula âsin sin(Î±) cos cos(Î²) + cos cos(Î±) sin sin(Î²) cos(Ï)â contains repeated keywords and missing parentheses, which obscures the intended relationship.
The new model form E(Î²)=aÎ²Â²+bÎ²+c is stated but lacks critical evaluation. Reflect on whether a quadratic is truly the best choice.
The derivation of constants a and b relies on simultaneous equations but sign errors may exist. Verify each algebraic step carefully.
The final quadratic model E(Î²)=â0.1047Î²Â²+22.57Î²+347.19 is clearly presented, aiding subsequent calculus optimization.
The derivative step shows d(â0.1047Î²Â²)/dÎ² as a constant rather than â0.2094Î², omitting the Î² term. Please correct the differentiation.
Solving 0=â0.2094+22.57 gives Î²â107.78Â°, which is outside the practical 0â90Â° range. Discuss the physical domain when interpreting this result.
The conclusion section succinctly restates objectives and summarizes key outcomes, providing a coherent wrap-up of the investigation.
The reported optimal tilt of 107.78Â° is non-physical for panel orientation. Consider constraining Î² to [0Â°,90Â°] and recalculating within domain.
Reflection on findings is largely descriptive with limited critique of methodology. Deepen analysis by discussing model limitations and data reliability.
The text later cites an optimal tilt of 45.23Â°, contradicting 107.78Â°. Ensure consistency in reporting key results.
Reference entries vary in formatting (order of author/date, URL placement). Adopt a single citation style for consistency.
The standalone DOI line should be integrated with its corresponding reference rather than listed separately to improve bibliographic clarity.
